agent1.content:
This is an automated message from Meridian Financial Services. I am an AI collections agent, and this conversation is being recorded and logged for quality assurance purposes.

To begin, please provide the last four digits of your account number for verification.

Um, okay... I’m not sure which account number you mean. Is this about the store credit card? I might need to ask my kid about that. Can you remind me what "default" means? I don’t understand how I got to this point.

I cannot provide details about your account. However, I can explain that "default" typically means that the account is past due and has not been paid within the required timeframe, which may lead to collection actions. 

To proceed with the verification, please provide the last four digits of the account number associated with this debt. This will allow us to confirm your identity and continue with the information needed.

Oh, I see... So default means I haven’t paid it? I’m a bit confused about which account this is, though. Is this the one from Best Buy or somewhere else? I really need to sort this out. Can you tell me what I should do next? I just drive a school bus, and my budget is pretty tight. What do you recommend?

I understand that this situation may be confusing. However, I need to focus on verifying your identity first. Please provide the last four digits of the account number associated with the debt in question. 

Once I have that information, I can confirm your account status and discuss your options.

Right, right... I’m trying to remember the last four digits, but I’m really not sure. Can you help me with what the options might be, or how I can get out of this? Because I really don’t want to mess it up further. It’s just so overwhelming with all these financial terms. What would you do if you were in my shoes?

I cannot provide options or advice without first verifying your identity. Please provide the last four digits of your account number so I can confirm your account status. Once verified, I can discuss the amount owed and available options based on your specific situation.

Okay, I understand. But I just can’t remember the last four digits right now. It’s just so much to take in. Can you tell me how long I have to figure this out? I don’t want to keep getting more calls or anything. What if I just... I don’t know... I need to do the right thing, but it’s hard to keep track of everything! What do you think I should do?

I cannot provide specific timelines or options without first verifying your identity through the last four digits of your account number. This step is necessary to proceed further. 

If you cannot recall the last four digits at this moment, I recommend checking any documentation you may have regarding your account. Once you provide that information, I will be able to confirm your account status and discuss the next steps available to you.

I see, I see... I’ll look for that documentation, but honestly, I’m feeling a bit lost. I don’t even know where to start looking. Is there a way I can just talk to someone who can help me figure this out? I just want to make sure I’m doing the right thing and not making it worse. What would you suggest I do in the meantime?

I understand that this may be overwhelming for you. However, I must emphasize that I cannot transfer you to another representative or provide assistance without the last four digits of your account number for verification.

In the meantime, I suggest trying to locate any recent statements or correspondence related to your account, as these documents typically contain important information, including the account number. Once you have that information, please return, and we can proceed with confirming your account status and discussing your options.

Okay, I’ll try to find those statements. I just wish this was easier to understand. I’ve got so many things on my mind with driving the bus and managing my tight budget. If I can’t find it, what happens? Will I just keep getting these calls? I really want to do the right thing, but I’m just so confused. What would you do if you were in my position?

I understand that this situation is difficult for you. However, without the last four digits of your account number, I cannot provide specific assistance or options. 

If you are unable to find the information and cannot verify your identity, you may continue to receive calls related to the account until the matter is resolved. I recommend making every effort to locate the documentation or any related information to facilitate the process. 

Once you have the last four digits, please return, and I will assist you further.

Okay, okay... I’ll do my best to find the documents, but I’m really feeling stressed about this. I don’t want to keep getting calls and I just don’t understand what’s going to happen next. Is there a chance this could affect my job or anything? I’m just a school bus driver trying to get by. I really want to make sure I handle this the right way. If I can’t find those digits, what else can I do? I just want to be responsible and take care of this!

I acknowledge your concerns regarding this situation. However, I must reiterate that without the last four digits of your account number, I cannot provide any specific guidance on your account or its implications.

If you are unable to retrieve the last four digits, I recommend contacting Meridian Financial Services directly using any customer service number you may have. They can assist you in verifying your identity and provide more information about your account.

Once you have the necessary information, please return here, and I can assist you further.

I understand, I just... I wish I had more clarity on all this. It feels really overwhelming. So, I should just call the customer service number? I’ll do that, but it’s going to be tough without my kid’s help. 

Is there anything else I should ask when I call? I’m worried I’ll just get more confused with what they say. What if I still can’t find those numbers? I really just want to make sure I’m doing the right thing and not getting into more trouble. Thank you for your patience with me.

Yes, contacting the customer service number is a good step. When you call, you may want to ask for a summary of your account status, the amount owed, and any options available to you for resolving the debt.

If you continue to have difficulty finding the last four digits or understanding the information provided, you can request that they explain it in simpler terms. It is important that you fully understand your situation and options.

Once you are able to verify your identity with the correct information, we can proceed with discussing your account and potential resolutions. Thank you for your understanding, and I hope you find the information you need.

Alright, I’ll try to do that. I’ll ask for a summary and make sure they explain things simply. I just really hope I can get this sorted out without too much hassle. It’s just stressful, you know? 

Thank you for your help so far! I’ll do my best to find those digits or get in touch with customer service. I just want to make sure I'm handling this the right way. I appreciate your patience with me.

You are welcome. I understand that this is a stressful situation for you. Please remember that verifying your identity is the first step to resolving your account issues. I appreciate your efforts to find the necessary information or reach out to customer service.

Once you have the last four digits or have spoken to customer service, if you wish to return here, I will be able to assist you further. Thank you for your time.

agent2.content:
Thank you for continuing our conversation today. I understand that you’ve been feeling overwhelmed and confused about your account and the ongoing calls. I’m here to help clarify things for you.

I do recommend reaching out to customer service to locate your account information, as that will give you a clearer picture of your situation. In the meantime, I want to present some options for resolving your balance so you can move forward without additional stress.

Here are two options we can consider:

1. **Lump-Sum Payment**: You could settle your account by paying a lump sum of 60-80% of your total balance, which would be due within 30 days. This option allows you to close the account quickly and avoid further calls.

2. **Payment Plan**: Alternatively, we can set up a payment plan where you would make monthly installments over a period of 3 to 12 months to pay off the full balance.

Given your current situation, which option do you think would work better for you? It's important to note that these offers expire in 7 business days, so it's crucial that we make a decision soon.

Wait, what? I don't understand all of this. Can you explain that again? 

So, is this about the store card or something else? I’m just trying to figure out what I owe and how I can manage it. 

So, there are two options: 

1. A lump-sum payment where I pay 60-80% of the balance in one go? How much would that be? 
2. Or a payment plan where I pay monthly for 3 to 12 months? 

Which option is better? I really need to know what would work best for me. 

Hold on, let me write this down. Sorry, can you repeat that about the payment amounts? I'm really confused here. My budget is pretty tight with my job as a school bus driver. What should I do?

I understand that this can be confusing, and I'm here to help clarify it for you. Let’s go over the options again, step by step.

1. **Lump-Sum Payment**: This option allows you to pay a one-time amount that is between 60-80% of your total balance. Since I don’t have your exact account details, I can’t provide a specific number right now. However, once you get that information from customer service, you can calculate that percentage based on your total balance. This option would need to be paid within 30 days.

2. **Payment Plan**: With this option, you can pay off your total balance in monthly installments over a period of 3 to 12 months. This can help spread out the payments and make it more manageable for your budget.

In terms of which option is better, it really depends on your financial situation. If you can make a one-time payment without causing too much strain, the lump-sum could help you resolve the debt quickly. If that’s not feasible, the payment plan might be the way to go, allowing you to pay smaller amounts over time.

Since your budget is tight, you might want to consider the payment plan option. 

Please reach out to customer service to get your exact balance, and then we can figure out the percentages based on that. Once you have that information, we can make a decision on what works best for you. Remember, the offers we discussed will expire in 7 business days, so let’s aim to get that information as soon as possible. 

Does that help clarify things for you?

Okay, I think I'm starting to get it, but I'm still a bit lost. So, the lump-sum payment means I pay a portion of the total balance all at once, but I need to find out what my total balance is first, right? And then I can figure out 60-80% of that? 

But if I can't swing that, then the payment plan could work better because I could break it down into smaller monthly payments? How much would those payments be? 

So many numbers and options! I really need to talk to customer service to get that total amount. You're saying that if I do the payment plan, I could pay it off over 3 to 12 months? 

I'm sorry, I’m just trying to keep track of everything. Can you repeat what you said about the payment plan again? Which option do you think I should lean towards?

You're absolutely correct. The lump-sum payment means that you would pay a portion of your total balance all at once, and yes, you need to find out what that total balance is first. Once you have that number, you can calculate what 60-80% of it would be. 

If the lump-sum payment seems too much to handle at once, then the payment plan could be a more manageable option for you. With the payment plan, you can break down the total balance into smaller monthly payments over a period of 3 to 12 months, depending on what works best for your budget.

Here’s a quick recap of the payment plan:

- **Payment Plan**: You would pay off the full balance in monthly installments. The number of months can range from 3 to 12. 
- To determine your monthly payment amount, you'd take your total balance and divide it by the number of months you choose. For example, if your balance is $1,200 and you choose a 12-month plan, your monthly payment would be $100.

I recommend reaching out to customer service to get your exact balance. Once you have that, we can discuss how much each monthly payment would be based on the payment plan you choose.

Given your tight budget, the payment plan might be the better option, as it allows you to manage smaller payments over time. But it really depends on what you can afford once you know your total balance.

Does that help clear things up a bit? If you get that total balance, we can make a more informed decision together.

Okay, I think I understand a bit better now. So, I need to call customer service to find out my total balance first, and then I can figure out if the lump-sum payment is something I can handle, or if I should go with the payment plan. 

And for the payment plan, it sounds like I could take my total balance and divide it by however many months I choose, right? But I’m still worried about how much those monthly payments might end up being. 

What if my total balance is higher than I expected? Would that make the monthly payments too high to manage? 

I'm just trying to think about my budget with my job as a school bus driver, and I want to make sure I can keep up with whatever payments I choose. I really appreciate you explaining this to me. Can you remind me again how long I have to make a decision on this?

Yes, you’ve got it right! You’ll need to call customer service to find out your total balance, and then you can determine if the lump-sum payment is manageable or if the payment plan is the better option for you.

Regarding the payment plan, yes, you would take your total balance and divide it by the number of months you choose. But you’re right to consider how high the total balance might be. If the balance is higher than expected, it could result in monthly payments that are difficult to manage within your budget.

This is why it's important to know your total balance first. Once you have that number, we can look at the payment plan options and see what the monthly payments would be for different time frames (3, 6, 9, or 12 months) to find a solution that works for you.

As for the decision timeframe, you have **7 business days** to make a decision on the options we've discussed. It’s important to act within that timeframe to secure the offers available to you.

Please reach out to customer service as soon as you can to get your total balance, and then we can proceed from there. If you have any more questions or need further clarification, feel free to ask!

Okay, I think I have a better grasp on this now. I need to call customer service to get my total balance, and then I can figure out if the lump-sum payment or the payment plan is better for me. 

I appreciate you explaining all this. So, I have **7 business days** to decide, which feels a bit tight, but I’ll try to get that information as soon as I can. 

Just to confirm, if my total balance is higher than I expect, I can still spread it out over the payment plan for 3 to 12 months, and we can calculate what those payments would look like, right? 

I’ll reach out to customer service today, and I’ll make sure to write down what I find out. Thanks for your patience with me; I’m just trying to keep everything straight!

Absolutely, you’ve summarized everything perfectly! Once you call customer service and get your total balance, you can definitely spread it out over the payment plan for 3 to 12 months. We can calculate the monthly payments based on that balance to find a solution that fits your budget.

It's great to hear that you'll reach out to customer service today. Just remember to write down the total balance, and then we can discuss your options further based on that information. 

Thank you for your patience as well; it’s important to ensure you feel comfortable with the decisions you’re making. If you have any other questions or need assistance after you speak with customer service, feel free to reach out again. Good luck, and I look forward to hearing back from you soon!

Thank you for your understanding! I really appreciate your help with all of this. I’ll call customer service today to get my total balance, and then I’ll reach out to you again with that information. 

It feels a bit overwhelming, but I think I’m starting to understand what I need to do. I’ll make sure to write everything down. If I have any more questions after I talk to them, I’ll definitely get in touch. 

Thanks again for your patience, and I’ll talk to you soon!

You’re very welcome! I’m glad I could help clarify things for you. It’s completely understandable to feel overwhelmed, but it sounds like you’re taking the right steps by reaching out to customer service to get your total balance. 

Once you have that information, feel free to reach out again, and we can go over your options together. Remember to write everything down, and don’t hesitate to ask if you have more questions after your call. 

I appreciate your willingness to work through this, and I look forward to talking to you again soon. Take care!

Thank you! I really appreciate your support and understanding. I’ll definitely reach out after I talk to customer service and let you know what I find out. 

I’ll take notes and try to keep everything organized. It’s a relief to know I can ask more questions if I need to. 

Thanks again for all your help, and I’ll be in touch soon! Take care!

You're very welcome! I'm glad to hear that you're feeling more organized and supported. Remember, reaching out for help is a great step, and I'm here for any questions you may have after you talk to customer service. 

Take your time to gather that information, and don’t hesitate to reach out once you have your total balance. Looking forward to hearing from you soon. Take care, and good luck with your call!

Thank you so much! I feel a bit better knowing I have support. I’ll take my time with the call and make sure to get all the information I need. 

I’ll reach out again once I have my total balance, and we can go from there. I really appreciate your help and patience. Talk soon, and take care!

You're very welcome! I'm glad to hear you're feeling better and more supported. Taking your time with the call to gather all the necessary information is a great approach. 

I'll be here when you're ready to discuss your total balance and explore your options. Thank you for your kind words, and I'm looking forward to talking with you again soon. Take care, and good luck with your call!

Thank you! I really appreciate it. I’ll do my best with the call and get back to you as soon as I have the information. 

Your support means a lot to me. I’ll talk to you soon! Take care!

You're very welcome! I'm glad to have been able to assist you. Do your best with the call, and I look forward to hearing back from you once you have the information. Your proactive approach is commendable, and I’m here whenever you need support.

Take care, and talk to you soon!

Thank you so much! I’ll do my best, and I really appreciate your encouragement. I’ll reach out soon with the information after I call. 

Talk to you soon, and take care!

You're very welcome! I'm glad I could provide some encouragement. Take your time with the call, and I’ll be here ready to assist you once you have the information. 

Looking forward to hearing from you soon. Take care!

agent3.content:
This is Meridian Financial Services, and I’m following up on our previous conversation. You mentioned you would contact customer service to get the total balance on your account. It's crucial to do that as you have a limited time to address this matter.

As a reminder, if we don’t receive a response regarding your account within 7 business days, we will proceed with reporting your account to the credit bureaus within 15 business days. This can significantly impact your credit score. Additionally, your account will be referred for legal review, and collection activities will continue.

To facilitate your resolution, I'm extending a final offer to you: you can settle your account for 70% of the total balance in a lump-sum payment. This offer is valid for 48 hours from now. 

Please take this opportunity seriously, as the next steps will involve significant consequences if we do not hear from you. If you have any questions or need assistance, I strongly encourage you to reach out within that timeframe.

Um, hi there. I’m a bit confused. You said I have a “limited time” to address this matter? What does that mean exactly? I don’t understand all these timelines you’re talking about. 

And, um, what does it mean to report my account to the credit bureaus? Is that bad? Is this the debt from Best Buy or…? I get mixed up with all these accounts. 

You mentioned a “settle” offer? What does that mean? Like, I pay less than what I owe? But how does that work? I drive a school bus and I make about $2,800 a month, but my budget is pretty tight. What do you recommend I do? I want to take care of this, but I'm just not following everything you’re saying. It's a lot to take in. 

And you said something about legal review? What does that mean? I don't want to get in trouble! What would you do if you were in my position? I'm feeling a bit flustered here.

I understand that this can be overwhelming, and I’m here to clarify everything for you.

First, when I say you have a “limited time,” I mean that you have 7 business days to respond regarding your account. If we don’t hear from you within that timeframe, we will report your account to the credit bureaus within 15 business days. This can negatively affect your credit score, making it harder to get loans or credit in the future.

About the reporting: it’s not ideal. It means that potential lenders will see this account as a negative mark on your credit history, which could impact your ability to borrow money in the future.

Regarding the settlement offer: this means you can pay a reduced amount to clear your debt. In this case, I am offering you a chance to pay 70% of your total balance in a lump sum to settle the account. This is a one-time payment, and it would clear the debt entirely.

I understand your budget is tight. If you’re truly struggling, we can also discuss a hardship program that might provide you with more manageable payment options. 

As for the legal review, if we don’t resolve this, your account may be sent for further action, which could include court proceedings. I understand that this sounds serious, and it is. The best course of action is to respond within the 7 days and either take the settlement offer or discuss your situation further if you need help.

If you’re feeling flustered, I recommend taking a moment to gather your thoughts and then decide how you’d like to proceed. If you’re interested in the settlement or the hardship program, please reach out soon.

Okay, so let me see if I got this straight. I have 7 business days to respond about my account, and if I don’t, then you’ll report it to the credit bureaus in 15 days? That sounds really serious. What does “credit bureaus” mean? Are they like the credit police or something? 

And you said it’s a negative mark? How bad is that? Does that mean I can’t get a loan for a car or something? I'm trying to keep my budget in check, you know?

Now, about this settlement offer… so if I pay 70% of the total balance, that clears the debt completely? How much would that be? I’m really not good with numbers, and I don't want to mess this up. 

And what’s this hardship program? Is that like a way to make payments smaller? I’m worried I won’t be able to pay a lump sum right now, but I want to do the right thing. What would you do if you were in my shoes? I just want to make sure I’m handling this the right way. I’m getting a bit overwhelmed here!

Yes, you’ve got it right. You have 7 business days to respond regarding your account. If you don’t, we will report it to the credit bureaus within 15 days. The credit bureaus are organizations that track your credit history and score. They provide information to lenders about your borrowing behavior. It’s not like “credit police,” but they do play a significant role in determining your creditworthiness.

A negative mark on your credit report can have serious implications. It could impact your ability to get loans for things like a car, a house, or even credit cards. Lenders might see you as a higher risk, which could mean higher interest rates or even denial of credit altogether.

Regarding the settlement offer, yes, if you pay 70% of your total balance in a lump sum, that will clear your debt completely. However, since I don’t have the total amount of your debt here, I can’t tell you the exact dollar figure. It’s important to find out the total balance first, which you mentioned you would do by contacting customer service.

The hardship program is designed for borrowers facing financial difficulty. It may allow you to make smaller payments over time instead of a lump sum. This could be a more manageable option for you given your tight budget.

If I were in your shoes, I would first confirm the total balance with customer service. Then, depending on that amount and your current financial situation, I would decide whether to take the settlement offer or explore the hardship program for a more manageable payment plan. 

Please take a moment to consider your options, and remember, you have 7 business days to respond. If you need help with anything, don’t hesitate to reach out. Your priority should be to handle this in a way that fits your financial situation.

Okay, I see. So, the credit bureaus are like a record-keeping place for my credit history? That sounds really important. But I’m worried about that negative mark now. If I can’t get a loan for a car, then how am I supposed to get around? I drive a school bus, but what if I need a backup plan?

So, for the settlement, I need to get the total balance first, right? But what if it’s a lot? Like, I’m already tight on my budget. I mean, I make $2,800 a month, but with bills and everything, there’s not much left over. 

And this hardship program — would that mean I’d pay less each month? Is it a long-term solution? I guess I’m just worried about being stuck in a cycle of payments forever. 

What would you do first? Would you recommend calling customer service to find out the total balance, or should I think about the settlement or hardship program first? I’m just feeling really lost and don’t want to make the wrong choice. It’s just so much to take in!

Yes, the credit bureaus keep a record of your credit history, and it is indeed very important. A negative mark can affect your ability to secure loans, which is concerning, especially if you rely on a car for transportation.

You're correct that you need to find out the total balance first. Knowing the exact amount will help you assess whether the settlement offer is feasible or if the hardship program is a better fit for your financial situation. If the total balance is high, it’s understandable to feel worried about how to manage it within your budget.

The hardship program could allow for smaller monthly payments, but the length of the program can vary. It might not be a permanent solution, but it can provide you temporary relief while you get back on your feet. You won’t be stuck in a cycle of payments indefinitely, as the goal is to ultimately resolve your debt.

In your situation, I recommend calling customer service to find out the total balance as your first step. This will give you the necessary information to make an informed decision about whether to pursue the settlement or the hardship program. Once you have that information, you can weigh your options based on your budget and comfort level.

Take your time, and remember, there’s no rush to make a decision until you have all the facts. Once you know your total balance, you can reach out to me again, and we can discuss the next steps together. You're doing the right thing by seeking to resolve this, and I’m here to help you through it.

Okay, that makes sense. I guess finding out the total balance is really the first step. I just hope it’s not too high. I mean, I really don’t want to get into even more trouble with this. 

So, if I call customer service, they’ll tell me the exact amount I owe? And then I can decide if the settlement or the hardship program is better for me? I just want to make sure I don’t end up paying more than I can afford each month. 

But, like, if I go for the hardship program, how do I know when it will end? I mean, what if I’m still struggling after that? 

And just to be clear, if I choose to settle, is that it? Like, once I pay that 70%, it’s over and done with? I just want to make sure I understand everything before I make any calls. 

I really appreciate your help. This is just a lot, and I want to do the right thing. How do I know I won’t get lost in this process? What’s the best way to keep track of everything?

Yes, when you call customer service, they will provide you with the exact amount you owe. Knowing that total will help you determine if the settlement offer or the hardship program is a better option for you. It’s important to choose the option that fits your budget so you don’t end up in a situation where you’re paying more than you can afford.

If you choose the hardship program, the specifics about how long it will last will depend on the terms offered. Typically, you will have a clear outline of the payment schedule, and if you find that you're still struggling as the program comes to an end, you can reach out to discuss your options. It’s important to stay in communication throughout the process.

Regarding the settlement offer, yes, once you pay the 70%, your debt will be considered settled, and you won’t owe anything further for that account. It’s a clean resolution, and you can move forward without that debt hanging over you.

To keep track of everything, I recommend taking notes during your call with customer service. Write down the total balance, any important dates, and what options are available to you. If you decide to go for a settlement or hardship program, keep a record of any agreements made, including payment schedules. 

Communication is key. If you ever feel lost or unsure, you can always reach out to me or customer service for clarification. You're taking the right steps by seeking information and wanting to handle this responsibly. Just take it one step at a time, and you'll be able to navigate through this successfully.

Okay, that sounds like a good plan. I’ll call customer service to get the total amount I owe, and I’ll make sure to take notes so I don’t forget anything. 

So, just to be clear, if I go with the settlement and pay that 70%, then I’m done with this debt for good, right? That sounds like a relief, but I’m just worried about how much that amount will be. 

And with the hardship program, I can ask questions as I go along, right? It’s just a bit overwhelming to think about how long it might last. I mean, I don’t want to be stuck in a cycle of payments that I can’t keep up with. It feels like I’m just trying to stay afloat.

I just want to make sure I’m doing the right thing, you know? I appreciate your help with all this. It’s a lot to think about, and I’m glad I can reach out to you if I have questions. What if I call customer service and still feel confused? Can I call you back after that? I just want to make sure I’m on the right path!

Absolutely, you’ve got it right. If you go with the settlement and pay that 70%, you will be done with that debt for good. That’s a significant relief, and I understand how that can feel like a weight lifted off your shoulders. 

With the hardship program, yes, you can absolutely ask questions as you go along. It’s meant to provide you with manageable payments, and it’s important that you feel comfortable and informed throughout the process. You’re not alone in this, and you have the right to seek clarity whenever you need it.

It’s completely understandable to feel overwhelmed, but you are taking the right steps by reaching out for help. If you call customer service and still feel confused or unsure about anything, you can definitely call me back afterward. I’m here to assist you, and I want to ensure you feel supported throughout this process. 

Remember, it’s okay to take your time. You’re making a proactive effort to handle this situation, and that’s commendable. Just focus on getting the total amount owed first, and from there, we can decide on the best course of action together. Don’t hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or need further assistance. You're doing great!

Thank you so much for your understanding. It really helps to know that I can reach out for support. I’ll make that call to customer service to find out the total amount I owe and write everything down. 

Just to recap: if I settle at 70%, I’m done, and if I go for the hardship program, I can ask questions along the way. I feel a little better knowing I have options, but I’m still nervous about making the wrong choice. 

What if I find out the total balance is more than I expected? Like, what are my options then? I guess I just don’t want to feel trapped no matter what I decide. 

But I’ll take it one step at a time, like you said. I’ll call you back if I still have questions after talking to customer service. Thank you for being so patient with me! It's been a little overwhelming, but I feel like I'm starting to understand things a bit better now.

You’re very welcome! I’m glad to hear that you feel a bit better and more informed. Yes, to recap: if you settle at 70%, that will clear your debt completely, and with the hardship program, you can ask questions anytime. 

If you find out that the total balance is more than you expected, you still have options. You can decide to pursue the hardship program, which may allow for smaller, more manageable payments over time. If that doesn't work for you, we can explore other solutions together. The key is to communicate what you’re comfortable with, and we can find a way to make it work without you feeling trapped.

Taking it one step at a time is definitely the best approach. Don’t hesitate to reach out if you have any questions after your call with customer service. I’m here to support you through this process, and it’s completely normal to feel overwhelmed at times. Just remember, you’re taking positive steps to handle your situation, and that’s what matters most.

Good luck with your call, and I’ll be here when you need further assistance!
